evidence_citations.excerpt: I'm terrified of letting the family down; if I don't land a job, I feel like I'm failing them.
The fear that I won’t land anything fast keeps me in this loop of delay
I keep pushing that decision off
I keep finding new things to tweak and end up not finishing it

transcript.text: I'm stuck on whether to apply for that remote role I saw last week. The mortgage deadline is looming and I keep pushing that decision off. I need to figure out a plan before the house payment.

With the mortgage deadline looming, it's understandable that you're feeling stuck on whether to apply for that remote role. Taking the first step can be daunting, but it might help to break down the application process into smaller tasks. Why not start by updating your resume this week, and then you can build from there? This way, you'll be making progress without feeling overwhelmed by the entire application process at once.
I did start updating my resume, but I keep finding new things to tweak and end up not finishing it. I feel like I'm stuck in a loop of waiting for the perfect version before I can actually apply.

You're getting caught up in making your resume perfect, and it's stopping you from finishing it. I can see how that would be frustrating, especially with the mortgage deadline looming. What's the one thing that's holding you back from considering your resume 'good enough' to apply for the remote role?

I keep looping on tweaks because I can't accept the resume as 'good enough'; every time I think it's done, I spot something else to improve. That delay is exhausting, especially with the mortgage due and my spouse back at work next week. I don't want to risk sending a subpar resume, but the endless edits are making me feel stuck.

It's really tough to stop tweaking when you feel like your resume's not quite there, especially with a big deadline like the mortgage payment looming. You're feeling exhausted from all the edits, and it's understandable that you don't want to risk sending a subpar resume. Can you tell me more about what's driving your need for perfection in this - is it the fear of not getting the job, or something else?

I think it's because I'm terrified of letting the family down; if I don't land a job, I feel like I'm failing them. That fear keeps me tweaking the resume until I'm exhausted. I know technically I could just send it, but the anxiety keeps me stuck.

The fear of not being able to provide for your family is really weighing on you, and it's making it hard for you to feel okay with sending in a resume that's not perfect. It's like that fear is holding you back from taking the next step, even though you know you need to move forward. Yeah, it's tough to separate your self-worth from your ability to get a job, but it sounds like that's what's happening here. Can you think of a time when you've had to make a decision without knowing the outcome, and how you handled that uncertainty?

I keep thinking back to the time we had to decide whether to pivot the product before any revenue; we just went with the first idea and it worked out. Now I’m stuck putting off applying for any jobs, caught in the same loop. The mortgage deadline and my spouse coming back to work are adding a lot of pressure.

I can see how the pressure of the mortgage deadline and your spouse going back to work is really weighing on you, especially with the added fear of not being able to provide for your family. It's like that fear is holding you back from taking the next step, even though you know you need to move forward. You've had experiences in the past where you've made decisions without knowing the outcome, like with the product pivot, and it worked out. Yeah, it's tough to separate your self-worth from your ability to get a job, but it sounds like that's what's happening here. How do you think you could apply some of that same decision-making mindset to your current job search?

I could set a 30‑minute sprint to just hit send, but the mortgage is due next week and I’m still stuck putting off the application.  The fear that I won’t land anything fast keeps me in this loop of delay.  If I treat it like a sprint, maybe I’ll get somewhere before the house payment hits.

You're feeling stuck in this loop of delay, and the fear of not landing a job quickly is really weighing on you. It's like that fear is holding you back from taking the next step, even though you know you need to move forward. Setting a 30-minute sprint to just hit send could be a great way to break through this, and it might help to remind yourself that it's okay to take things one step at a time. What do you think would happen if you did take that first step and hit send, even if it's not perfect?

I could try the 30‑minute sprint, but I'm still uneasy about sending a resume that feels unfinished, and the mortgage deadline is looming. If I send it now, I might still have to tweak it later, which feels like more work.

I can see that you're still feeling really uneasy about sending in your resume, even with the idea of a 30-minute sprint to just hit send. It's like the fear of it not being perfect is holding you back, and the looming mortgage deadline is adding to that pressure. Yeah, it's tough to separate your self-worth from your ability to get a job, but it sounds like that's what's happening here. What if you were to send in your resume as-is, and then use any feedback you get as a way to refine it for the next application? Sometimes, taking that first step can help build momentum and make the next steps feel less daunting.

I know sending it as‑is is a good idea, but I keep worrying about the mortgage coming up and my spouse coming back to work. The idea of just pushing it out feels like I'm not moving forward at all. Maybe if I could just get it out the door, the rest will feel a little less overwhelming.

You're feeling really stuck right now, and the pressure of the mortgage deadline and your spouse going back to work is weighing heavily on you. It sounds like sending in your resume as-is feels like a big risk, but at the same time, you're worried that not taking action will leave you feeling overwhelmed. Maybe taking that first step and getting the resume out the door could help you feel like you're moving forward, even if it's not perfect. What's the smallest step you could take today to start feeling like you're making progress on your job search?

I think the smallest thing I can do today is set a 15‑minute timer, open the resume, and just hit send on the version I have now—no more tweaking.  That way I’ll at least have a concrete action toward a new job, even if it’s not perfect.  The idea of actually sending it feels like a small win against the anxiety of the mortgage deadline and the upcoming return to work for my spouse.

That's a huge step, hitting send on your resume, even if it's not perfect. It's like you're taking a small win against the anxiety that's been holding you back. Yeah, it's still scary, but you're moving forward, and that's something to build on. What do you think will happen after you take this first step, and how will you use that momentum to keep going?

If I send it, I expect a response in a week or two and can set up a quick 30‑minute follow‑up call. I’ll block out the first two hours tomorrow for networking calls so I’m not stuck waiting for something else. The idea is to keep the momentum and not slip back into the loop of tweaking. I’ll treat each interview as a data point to decide my next move.

You've made some great progress in the last little while, and it's awesome that you're thinking about how to keep that momentum going. Blocking out time for networking calls tomorrow is a great idea, and treating each interview as a way to gather more information can help you feel more in control. What do you think is the most important thing for you to focus on during those networking calls, and how can you use them to build on the progress you've made so far?

turn_traces.user_message: I'm stuck on whether to apply for that remote role I saw last week. The mortgage deadline is looming and I keep pushing that decision off. I need to figure out a plan before the house payment.

candidate_bundle.intent_entry_style: Propose ONE small step matched to TTM stage. Don't deflect with a question.

common_candidates.label: Support
common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm a supportive companion to this person, and they need a plan to move forward with a job application. Given their cautious and anxious personality, I'll propose a small, manageable step. The evidence suggests they're feeling overwhelmed by the looming mortgage deadline and the pressure of making a decision. To help them feel more in control, I can suggest breaking down the application process into smaller tasks. My entry point will be to acknowledge the stress of the deadline and the importance of taking action.

response.final_response: I can see that you're still feeling really uneasy about sending in your resume, even with the idea of a 30-minute sprint to just hit send. Your plan to block out time for networking calls and treat each interview as a data point shows that you're thinking ahead and trying to stay proactive. My entry point will be to acknowledge the progress you've made so far and the anxiety you're still facing, and then help you explore ways to keep moving forward.

open and seeking guidance
blocking out time for networking calls and treating each interview as a data point

response.final_response: You've made some great progress in the last little while, and it's awesome that you're thinking about how to keep that momentum going. Blocking out time for networking calls tomorrow is a great idea, and treating each interview as a way to gather more information can help you feel more in control. What do you think is the most important thing for you to focus on during those networking calls, and how can you use them to build on the progress you've made so far?
